title: Blastomycosis
authors: Gregory M. Gauthier, Bruce S. Klein

items.title: Thermal Dimorphism of Blastomyces Species
items.content: Blastomyces exhibits thermal dimorphism, allowing it to switch between hyphal and yeast forms depending on temperature. This adaptation is crucial for its pathogenicity.
keyPoints: In soil (22–25°C), Blastomyces grows as septate hyphae producing infectious conidia.
At body temperature (37°C), it converts to a pathogenic yeast form causing pyogranulomatous inflammation.
The yeast forms exhibit broad-based budding and vary in size (4–29 μm) among species.

items.title: Species Complex of Blastomyces
items.content: Blastomyces comprises several species, with B. dermatitidis being the most clinically relevant due to its human pathogenicity.
keyPoints: The complex includes B. dermatitidis, B. gillchristii, B. helicus, B. percursus, B. emzantsi, B. silverae, and B. parvus.
B. silverae and B. parvus are not commonly known to infect humans.
B. helicus is unique for its coiled hyphal growth pattern and lack of sporulation in vitro.

items.title: Geographic Distribution and Species Variation of Blastomyces
items.content: Blastomycosis is primarily found in North America, with B. dermatitidis prevalent throughout its traditional range, while B. gilchristii is restricted to formerly glaciated areas. In Africa, multiple species including a newly identified B. emzantsi exist.
keyPoints: Traditional geographic range for Blastomyces includes Mississippi and Ohio River basins, St. Lawrence River basin, states bordering the Great Lakes, southeastern U.S., and provinces of Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, Quebec in Canada.
B. dermatitidis is found throughout its traditional range; B. gilchristii is restricted to formerly glaciated areas.
In Africa, multiple species exist including B. dermatitidis, B. gilchristii, B. percusus, and a newly identified B. emzantsi.

items.title: Epidemiologic Challenges in Understanding Blastomycosis
items.content: The epidemiology of blastomycosis is limited by the lack of sensitive diagnostic tests and difficulty in isolating the pathogen, leading to reliance on passive surveillance and retrospective studies.
keyPoints: Lack of sensitive skin testing and serologic testing hinders detailed epidemiologic understanding.
Blastomyces is difficult to isolate from the environment by culture or molecular methods.
Epidemiologic data primarily comes from passive laboratory surveillance, retrospective studies, and outbreak investigations.

items.title: Incidence Rates and Reporting of Blastomycosis
items.content: Blastomycosis incidence varies by region, with hyperendemic areas like Vilas County, Wisconsin, showing high rates. It is reportable in specific U.S. states and Canadian provinces.
keyPoints: Annual incidence ranges from 0.11 to 2.17 cases/100,000 persons in endemic areas; higher in older populations at 0.7/100,000.
Hyperendemic regions like Vilas County, Wisconsin, have rates of 40 to 117 cases/100,000 persons.
Reportable in Minnesota, Wisconsin, Michigan, Arkansas, Louisiana (U.S.) and Manitoba, Ontario (Canada).

items.title: Increased Risk of Blastomycosis in Hmong Population
items.content: Persons of Hmong ethnicity have an increased risk of acquiring blastomycosis, potentially due to genetic polymorphisms affecting IL-6 production and subsequent immune response.
keyPoints: 45% of patients in a Marathon County outbreak were of Hmong ethnicity.
14.4% of patients with blastomycosis at Marshfield Clinic were Asian, despite <2.5% of the population being Asian.
Polymorphisms in IL-6 gene lead to decreased IL-6 production and impaired development of IL-17-producing CD4+ T lymphocytes.
IL-17 is crucial for recruiting innate immune cells against Blastomyces.

items.title: Demographic Patterns in Blastomycosis Incidence
items.content: Increased incidence rates of blastomycosis are observed among indigenous people and Asians, who tend to be younger and have fewer underlying medical conditions compared to Caucasians.
keyPoints: Higher incidence rates reported in indigenous populations of Canada and the US.
Asian and indigenous persons with blastomycosis are generally younger and have fewer comorbidities than Caucasians.

items.title: Temperature-Dependent Morphologic Switch in Blastomyces
items.content: Blastomyces species exhibit thermal dimorphism, transitioning from hyphal to yeast form at mammalian core temperature (37°C). This switch is crucial for virulence and involves the uptake of exogenous cysteine to restart mitochondrial respiration.
keyPoints: Temperature shift from environmental to 37°C triggers morphologic change from hyphae to yeast.
Cysteine uptake is essential for completing the transition to yeast form.
Disruption of DRK1 gene results in avirulent cells that remain as hyphae at 37°C.

items.title: Role of BAD1 in Blastomyces Virulence
items.content: BAD1 encodes a multifunctional protein essential for virulence, facilitating yeast adherence to lung epithelial cells and modulating immune responses by interacting with host receptors.
keyPoints: BAD1 is upregulated during the transition to yeast phase at 37°C.
The 120-KDa BAD1 protein aids in adherence via heparin sulfate interaction and attaches to CR3 and CD14 complement receptors.
Deletion of BAD1 results in avirulence in murine models.

items.title: Additional Virulence Factors in Blastomyces Yeast
items.content: Blastomyces yeast exhibits resistance to oxidative stress and modulates immune responses through various mechanisms, contributing to its virulence.
keyPoints: Resistance to oxidative stress is enhanced by upregulation of catalase and superoxide dismutase.
Active zinc uptake via PRA1-encoded ZRT1 transporter supports yeast survival.
Dipeptidyl peptidase IVA cleaves granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor, impairing immune cell activation.

items.title: Why consider Blastomyces in differential diagnosis?
items.content: Blastomycosis can mimic various diseases including bacterial pneumonia, tuberculosis, and lung cancer. Awareness of its presentation is crucial for early diagnosis.
keyPoints: 50% of exposed individuals develop symptomatic infection after 3 weeks to 3 months.
Mimics infectious (e.g., bacterial pneumonia) and noninfectious diseases (e.g., skin cancer, sarcoidosis).
Consider Blastomyces in differential diagnosis for unresponsive pneumonia or extrapulmonary manifestations like skin lesions.
Environmental risk factors present in 50-60% of patients; exposure history is critical.

items.title: How to identify blastomycosis through clinical clues?
items.content: Identify blastomycosis by recognizing pneumonia unresponsive to antibiotics, extrapulmonary manifestations, and detailed exposure history.
keyPoints: Pneumonia not improving with antibiotics suggests possible blastomycosis.
Extrapulmonary manifestations include skin lesions, osteomyelitis, CNS involvement.
Consider in patients from endemic areas with unexplained respiratory failure or ARDS.
7.7-10% of patients have a pet with blastomycosis; 4-9% have a family member affected.

items.title: Pulmonary Blastomycosis: Prevalence and Severity
items.content: Pulmonary blastomycosis is the most common clinical feature of symptomatic infection, occurring in 69–93% of patients. It can present as asymptomatic or with a range of symptoms from influenza-like illness to ARDS.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 69–93% of symptomatic cases
Symptoms: fever, chills, cough, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, malaise, decreased appetite
Radiographic findings: lobar consolidation, mass lesion, interstitial infiltrates, nodules, miliary pattern, cavitary disease, diffuse involvement
Risk factors for severe disease: diabetes, solid organ transplant, immunosuppression, multilobar pneumonia
ARDS occurrence: 4–15% of pulmonary cases
Mortality in ARDS: 40 to 89%, increased with delayed diagnosis

items.title: Disseminated Blastomycosis: Common Sites and Risk Factors
items.content: Disseminated blastomycosis occurs in 15–48% of patients, commonly affecting the skin and bones. CNS involvement is less common but significant when present.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 15–48% of cases
Common sites: skin (papules, ulcers, verrucous lesions, abscesses), bone (osteomyelitis)
Bone infection details: typically involves a single bone; common sites include spine, long bones, ribs
CNS involvement: <10%, often with concurrent organ involvement
Risk factors for dissemination: B. dermatitidis more likely to disseminate than B. gilchristii
Immunosuppression's minimal influence on dissemination suggests virulence factors play a larger role

items.title: Blastomycosis in Special Populations: AIDS and Pregnancy
items.content: Patients with AIDS and low CD4+ T lymphocyte counts are at increased risk for CNS dissemination. Blastomycosis during pregnancy is typically diagnosed in the second or third trimester.
keyPoints: AIDS patients with CD4+ <100/μL: increased risk for CNS dissemination
Pregnancy: blastomycosis commonly diagnosed in 2nd or 3rd trimester (91%)
Manifestations during pregnancy: pneumonia (74%), disseminated infection (48%)
Transmission to neonate: rare, by transplacental route or aspiration of infected secretions

items.title: Why Suspect Blastomycosis?
items.content: Blastomycosis should be considered when patients present with symptoms mimicking common conditions such as community-acquired pneumonia, malignancy, or tuberculosis. A high degree of clinical suspicion is necessary due to its nonspecific laboratory findings.
keyPoints: Clinical and radiographic presentations mimic more common etiologies like pneumonia, malignancy, and tuberculosis.

items.title: How to Diagnose Blastomycosis?
items.content: Diagnosis involves microscopic examination of stained specimens, fungal culture, and antigen testing. The poor sensitivity of complement fixation (9%) and immunodiffusion (28%) makes serologic tests less useful.
keyPoints: Microscopic examination for broad-based budding yeast with a doubly refractile cell wall is crucial.
Sensitivity of staining of respiratory samples ranges from 50 to 90%.
A recently developed serologic test has a sensitivity of 87% and specificity of 94–99%.

items.title: When to Use Antigen Testing for Blastomycosis?
items.content: Antigen testing, which detects a conserved galactomannan component in the cell wall, is preferred over serologic tests. It can be performed on various samples including urine and blood.
keyPoints: Sensitivity of antigen test: 85–93% for urine, 57–82% for serum.
Cross-reactions occur with other dimorphic fungi like H. capsulatum (96%), Paracoccidioides species (100%).
Antigen levels can be used to monitor response to antifungal therapy.

items.title: Treatment Guidelines for Blastomycosis
items.content: All patients with blastomycosis should receive treatment to prevent progression of the disease, respiratory failure, and dissemination. Treatment regimens vary based on severity, immune status, CNS involvement, pregnancy, and comorbidities.
keyPoints: Treatment is recommended for all cases due to potential severe outcomes.
Antifungal selection depends on factors like immune status, CNS involvement, and drug interactions.
Amphotericin B (AmB) formulations and triazoles are effective; echinocandins are not.
For mild/moderate pulmonary or disseminated blastomycosis in immunocompetent patients: Itraconazole for 6 months.
Severe cases require induction with lipid AmB for 7–14 days, followed by itraconazole for 6–12 months.
CNS involvement requires lipid AmB for 4-6 weeks, then voriconazole, itraconazole, or fluconazole for at least 12 months.

items.title: Considerations in Special Populations
items.content: Treatment strategies for blastomycosis must be tailored to special populations such as immunosuppressed patients, pregnant women, and those with specific comorbidities.
keyPoints: Immunosuppressed patients: 7–14 days of lipid AmB followed by 12 months of itraconazole.
Pregnant women should receive lipid AmB for 6-8 weeks due to teratogenic risks associated with triazoles.
Fluconazole can cause craniofacial, skeletal, and cardiac defects; voriconazole and posaconazole may result in skeletal abnormalities.
Itraconazole increases the risk of spontaneous abortion. Pregnancy test recommended before starting therapy.

items.title: Antifungal Drug Efficacy and Considerations
items.content: The choice of antifungal drugs for blastomycosis is influenced by their efficacy against different Blastomyces species, patient-specific factors, and drug administration considerations.
keyPoints: Voriconazole, posaconazole, and isavuconazonium sulfate are potent alternatives to itraconazole.
These agents also show good activity against newly identified Blastomyces species like B. helicus, B. percursus, and B. emzantsi.
Fluconazole has higher MICs against B. percursus and B. emzantsi compared to other triazoles.
Itraconazole capsules require an acidic environment for absorption; solution form can be used with gastric acid-lowering therapies.

items.title: Mortality Rates and Risk Factors in Blastomycosis
items.content: Blastomycosis has a mortality rate ranging from 5 to 13%, with most deaths due to respiratory failure from ARDS. Hospital data shows a 7.9% mortality rate, higher among older patients or those with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease or malignancy.
keyPoints: Mortality rates for blastomycosis range from 5 to 13%
Most deaths are associated with respiratory failure due to ARDS
Hospital data (2010-2020) shows a 7.9% mortality rate
Increased mortality in older patients or those with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease or concomitant malignancy

items.title: Challenges and Advances in Blastomycosis Prevention
items.content: Prevention of blastomycosis is difficult due to sporadic, unpredictable environmental acquisition. Research on a live, attenuated vaccine strain deficient in BAD1 shows promise by inducing immunity via T_H17 lymphocytes activation.
keyPoints: Prevention is challenging due to sporadic and unpredictably acquired infections
Live, attenuated vaccine strain deficient in BAD1 induces sterilizing immunity in mice
Vaccine activates T_H17 lymphocytes for protection against pulmonary challenge
Major antigens include calnexin and Blastomyces endoglucanase-2

items.question: Which of the following Blastomyces species is known for its unique coiled hyphal growth and lack of sporulation under in vitro conditions?
items.explanation: B. helicus is unique because its hyphae grow in a coiled pattern and it does not sporulate under in vitro growth conditions.

items.question: At what temperature range do Blastomyces species typically grow as septate hyphae producing infectious conidia?
items.explanation: In the soil (22–25°C), Blastomyces grows as septate hyphae that produce infectious conidia.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a species commonly known to infect humans?
items.explanation: B. silverae and B. parvus are not known to commonly infect humans.

items.question: Which geographic regions in North America have been identified as traditional endemic areas for Blastomyces infections?
options: The Mississippi and Ohio River basins, the St. Lawrence River basin, states bordering the Great Lakes, and southeastern states.
Only the provinces of Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, and Quebec in Canada.
Primarily urban areas throughout North America.
Exclusively rural areas along the western United States.
items.explanation: The traditional geographic range for Blastomyces includes specific river basins, states bordering the Great Lakes, and southeastern states in the U.S., as well as certain Canadian provinces.

items.question: Which species of Blastomyces is geographically restricted to formerly glaciated areas?
items.explanation: B. gilchristii is restricted to formerly glaciated areas, whereas B. dermatitidis can be found in both glaciated and nonglaciated areas.

items.question: In which North American locations has blastomycosis been identified as an emerging pathogen?
options: The Capitol District and upper Susquehanna River Subbasin in New York State, and the province of Saskatchewan.
Only in urban areas across the United States.
Primarily along the Mississippi River basin.
Exclusively within the St. Lawrence River basin.
items.explanation: Blastomycosis is emerging as a pathogen in upstate New York State and in Saskatchewan, Canada.

items.question: Which molecular techniques have been used to identify new Blastomyces species?
options: Internal transcribed spacer sequencing, multilocus sequence typing, and whole genome sequencing.
PCR amplification of ribosomal RNA genes only.
Southern blotting and restriction fragment length polymorphism analysis.
items.explanation: A combination of internal transcribed spacer (ITS) sequencing, multilocus sequence typing (MLST), and whole genome sequencing has been used to identify new Blastomyces species such as B. emzantsi.

items.question: Which genetic polymorphism is associated with an increased risk of acquiring blastomycosis in the Hmong population?
options: Polymorphisms in the CD4+ T lymphocyte gene
Polymorphisms in the neutrophil activation gene
items.explanation: The passage states that polymorphisms in the IL-6 gene result in decreased IL-6 production, impairing immune response and increasing blastomycosis risk.

items.question: What is a critical cytokine for recruitment and activation of innate immune cells against Blastomyces?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that IL-17 is critical for recruiting and activating innate immune cells like neutrophils and macrophages against Blastomyces.

items.question: Which population groups have been reported to have increased incidence rates of blastomycosis?
options: Indigenous people of Canada and the United States
Persons of Asian ethnicity in Europe
items.explanation: The passage mentions increased incidence rates among indigenous people of Canada and the United States, as well as persons of Hmong ethnicity.

items.question: Which factor is essential for the transition of Blastomyces from hyphae to yeast at $37^{	ext{o}}$C?
items.explanation: The uptake of exogenous cysteine is required to complete the transition to the yeast form by restarting mitochondrial respiration.

items.question: What role does BAD1 play in Blastomyces virulence?
options: Facilitates adherence to lung epithelial cells and immune modulation
items.explanation: BAD1 facilitates yeast adherence to lung epithelial cells and modulates immune responses, contributing to virulence.

items.question: What is the consequence of disrupting DRK1 in Blastomyces?
options: Cells grow as yeast at $37^{	ext{o}}$C and are highly virulent
Cells grow as hyphae at $37^{	ext{o}}$C and are avirulent
Increased production of TNF-α in macrophages
items.explanation: Disruption of DRK1 results in cells that grow as hyphae at $37^{	ext{o}}$C and are avirulent.

items.question: Which transcription factor is involved in the transition from yeast to hyphae?
items.explanation: The GATA-transcription factor encoded by SREB is involved in the transition from yeast to hyphae.

items.question: What is the estimated percentage of persons exposed to Blastomyces who develop symptomatic infection?
items.explanation: It is estimated that 50% of persons exposed to Blastomyces develop symptomatic infection.

items.question: Which condition can blastomycotic pneumonia clinically and radiographically resemble?
items.explanation: Blastomycotic pneumonia resembles community-acquired bacterial pneumonia.

items.question: What percentage of patients with blastomycosis have environmental risk factors?
items.explanation: Approximately 50–60% of patients will have environmental risk factors for blastomycosis.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common radiographic finding in pulmonary blastomycosis?
options: Distinctive features differentiating it from other pulmonary diseases
items.explanation: No distinctive features differentiate blastomycosis from other pulmonary diseases.

items.question: What is the mortality rate range for ARDS caused by pulmonary blastomycosis?
items.explanation: The mortality rate in ARDS is between 40 to 89%.

items.question: Which of the following factors has minimal influence on the dissemination of blastomycosis?
items.explanation: Immunosuppression has only a minimal influence on dissemination.

items.question: Which organism is more likely to cause disseminated infection in Wisconsin?
items.explanation: B. dermatitidis is more likely to cause disseminated infection in Wisconsin.

items.question: Which laboratory finding is NOT specific for diagnosing blastomycosis?
options: Broad-based budding yeast with a doubly refractile cell wall
items.explanation: Broad-based budding yeast is specific for diagnosing blastomycosis, unlike nonspecific findings like leukocytosis and increased CRP.

items.question: What is the sensitivity range of staining respiratory samples for Blastomyces?
items.explanation: The sensitivity of staining respiratory samples for Blastomyces ranges from 50 to 90%.

items.question: Which temperature is optimal for hyphal growth of Blastomyces in culture?
items.explanation: Blastomyces typically shows hyphal growth at incubation temperatures of 25–30°C.

items.question: Which test has supplanted serologic testing for diagnosing blastomycosis?
items.explanation: The antigen test detecting a conserved galactomannan component has supplanted serologic testing.

items.question: Which antifungal drug is ineffective for treating blastomycosis due to the minimal amount of beta-(1,3)-glucan in the Blastomyces yeast cell wall?
items.explanation: Echinocandins are ineffective against blastomycosis due to the minimal amount of beta-(1,3)-glucan in the Blastomyces yeast cell wall.

items.question: For immunocompetent patients with mild or moderate pulmonary or disseminated blastomycosis, what is the recommended treatment duration?
items.explanation: Itraconazole therapy for 6 months is recommended for immunocompetent patients with mild or moderate blastomycosis.

items.question: Which antifungal agent should not be used by persons taking antacids, H2 antagonists, or proton pump inhibitors due to absorption issues?
items.explanation: Itraconazole capsules require an acidic gastric environment for optimal absorption and should not be used with antacids, H2 antagonists, or proton pump inhibitors.

items.question: Which antifungal agent is recommended for treating blastomycosis in pregnant women due to its non-teratogenic nature?
items.explanation: Lipid Amphotericin B is recommended for treating blastomycosis in pregnant women because it is not teratogenic.

items.question: What is a common long-term outcome for patients who recover from pulmonary blastomycosis?
options: Development of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
No long-term loss of pulmonary function
Increased susceptibility to other fungal infections
items.explanation: The vast majority of patients who recover from pulmonary blastomycosis do not experience long-term loss of pulmonary function.

items.question: Which factor is associated with increased mortality in patients with blastomycosis?
items.explanation: Increased mortality is associated with older age, underlying chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, or concomitant malignancy.

items.question: What is a typical outcome of cutaneous blastomycosis?
options: Scarring at the site of infection
items.explanation: Cutaneous blastomycosis typically results in scarring.

items.statement: All Blastomyces species grow as broad-based budding yeast cells in human organs at body temperature.
items.explanation: In organs and tissues (37°C), all Blastomyces species grow as pathogenic yeast that elicits pyogranulomatous inflammation.

items.statement: B. parvus is a common cause of human blastomycosis.
items.explanation: B. silverae and B. parvus are not known to commonly infect humans.

items.statement: Blastomycosis is only endemic in North America.
items.explanation: Blastomycosis occurs globally, with cases reported in Africa, Asia, and other regions outside of North America.

items.statement: The majority of Blastomyces outbreaks have occurred in urban settings.
items.explanation: Most outbreaks have been in rural areas, although some have occurred in urban settings.

items.statement: Persons of Hmong ethnicity are at an increased risk for disseminated blastomycosis.
items.explanation: The passage states that there is no evidence suggesting increased risk for disseminated blastomycosis in the Hmong population.

items.statement: Persons of Asian and indigenous descent with blastomycosis tend to have more underlying medical conditions compared to Caucasians.
items.explanation: The passage notes that these groups tend to have fewer underlying medical conditions and are younger than Caucasians.

items.statement: BAD1 is present in all sequenced genomes of Blastomyces species.
items.explanation: BAD1 is absent from the sequenced genomes of B. helicus, B. parvus, B. silverae, B. percursus, and B. emzantsi.

items.statement: Blastomyces can infect both immunocompetent and immunocompromised persons.
items.explanation: Blastomyces is one of the few fungi that can infect immunocompetent individuals.

items.statement: Blastomycotic pneumonia can be mistaken for viral pneumonia.
items.explanation: Blastomycotic pneumonia clinically and radiographically resembles viral pneumonia.

items.statement: A detailed exposure history is unnecessary in the diagnosis of blastomycosis.
items.explanation: A detailed exposure history can elevate blastomycosis in the differential diagnosis.

items.statement: Hilar adenopathy, pleural effusion, and empyema are common findings in pulmonary blastomycosis.
items.explanation: Hilar adenopathy, pleural effusion, and empyema are uncommon.
In contrast to B. dermatitidis and B. gilchristii, B. helicus commonly causes fungemia.

items.statement: Serologic tests for Blastomyces have high sensitivity and are commonly used.
items.explanation: Complement fixation and immunodiffusion have poor sensitivities, rendering serologic testing diagnostically dispensable.

items.statement: Blastomyces can be definitively diagnosed by culture growth within 5–28 days.
items.explanation: Growth of Blastomyces in cultures provides a definitive diagnosis but requires 5–28 days.

items.statement: Fluconazole can be safely used during pregnancy to treat blastomycosis.
items.explanation: Fluconazole is teratogenic and can cause craniofacial, skeletal, and cardiac defects in the developing fetus.

items.statement: Itraconazole solution can be administered to patients on gastric acid-lowering therapies.
items.explanation: Itraconazole solution does not require an acidic gastric environment and can be given with gastric acid-lowering therapies.

items.statement: A live, attenuated vaccine strain deficient in BAD1 has been shown to induce sterilizing immunity against B. dermatitidis in mice.
items.explanation: The BAD1-null vaccine strain induces sterilizing immunity by activating TH17 lymphocytes in mice.

items.statement: Recombinant antigen-based vaccines for blastomycosis are commercially available.
items.explanation: Neither the BAD1-null attenuated vaccine nor recombinant antigen-based vaccines are commercially available.

items.reason: This involves the ability of these fungi to convert between hyphal and yeast morphologies in response to temperature changes.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, and the reason correctly explains it as Blastomyces exhibits thermal dimorphism by converting between forms based on temperature.

items.assertion: Blastomyces infections are primarily acquired through inhalation of infectious particles from disturbed soil.
items.reason: Activities such as construction, excavation, and water sports can disturb the soil and release infectious particles.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how infections are acquired.

items.assertion: The incidence data for blastomycosis likely overestimate the true burden of infection.
items.reason: Incidence data include asymptomatic or subclinical infections, which are not typically reported.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because incidence data underestimate the true burden due to underreporting of asymptomatic cases; the reason is also false.

items.assertion: Persons of Hmong ethnicity have an increased risk of acquiring blastomycosis.
items.reason: Polymorphisms in the IL-6 gene result in decreased IL-6 production, impairing immune response against Blastomyces.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why Hmong individuals have an increased risk.

items.assertion: The morphologic switch from hyphae to yeast at $37^{	ext{o}}$C is essential for Blastomyces virulence.
items.reason: DRK1 disruption results in cells that grow as hyphae at $37^{	ext{o}}$C and are avirulent.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the switch is essential for virulence.

items.assertion: Blastomyces requires BAD1 to grow as yeast at $37^{	ext{o}}$C.
items.reason: BAD1 encodes a protein that restarts mitochondrial respiration during the morphologic switch.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; BAD1 is not involved in restarting mitochondrial respiration.

items.assertion: Patients with AIDS and CD4+ T lymphocyte counts of <100/μL are at increased risk for CNS dissemination.
items.reason: Immunosuppression has a significant impact on the dissemination of blastomycosis in these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Blastomycosis is typically diagnosed in the first trimester of pregnancy.
items.reason: Pregnancy increases susceptibility to blastomycosis early on.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; blastomycosis is typically diagnosed in the second or third trimester.

items.assertion: The antigen test for blastomycosis can detect B. dermatitidis, B. gilchristii, and B. helicus.
items.reason: Cross-reactions in the antigen test occur with other dimorphic fungi like H. capsulatum.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, cross-reactions do not explain why the antigen test can detect specific Blastomyces species.

items.assertion: Blastomyces hyphae grown at 25–30°C are morphologically distinct enough to confirm diagnosis.
items.reason: Morphologic analysis of yeast by light microscopy differentiates among the species of Blastomyces.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Blastomyces hyphae are not morphologically distinct at 25–30°C, and the reason is also false as it does not differentiate species.

items.assertion: For severe blastomycosis, induction therapy with lipid AmB followed by itraconazole is recommended.
items.reason: Lipid AmB is used for initial treatment due to its effectiveness in severe cases, and itraconazole follows for maintenance therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Voriconazole has higher MICs against B. dermatitidis and B. gilchristii compared to fluconazole.
items.reason: Fluconazole's MIC is higher than that of voriconazole or itraconazole for these species.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; voriconazole has potent activity against B. dermatitidis and B. gilchristii, unlike fluconazole.
